buggy_code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

returns = pd.read_csv('portfolio_returns.csv', index_col='date')['returns']

# Parametric VaR assuming normal distribution
mean = returns.mean()
std = returns.std()

# 99% VaR assuming normality
var_99 = mean - 2.326 * std

print(f'1-day 99% VaR: {var_99:.4%}')
clean_code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

returns = pd.read_csv('portfolio_returns.csv', index_col='date')['returns']

# Historical simulation VaR — no distribution assumption
var_99_hist = returns.quantile(0.01)

# Conditional VaR (Expected Shortfall) — average of worst 1%
var_threshold = returns.quantile(0.01)
var_99_es = returns[returns <= var_threshold].mean()

print(f'1-day 99% Historical VaR: {var_99_hist:.4%}')
print(f'1-day 99% Expected Shortfall: {var_99_es:.4%}')
description: Value at Risk computed assuming normal distribution of returns.
explanation: Financial returns have fat tails — extreme losses occur much more frequently than a normal distribution predicts. The normal 99% VaR using 2.326 standard deviations systematically underestimates tail risk. In the 2008 financial crisis, many banks using normal VaR models suffered losses their models said were near-impossible.
suggested_fix: Use historical simulation (empirical quantile) or Expected Shortfall which doesn't assume normality.

issues.title: VaR computed assuming normal distribution understates tail risk
